mechanism: Measurement of perceived human attributes (agency, experience, emotions) is compared against behavioral outcomes (support for harm practices) across conditions. The underlying process is dichotomous categorization: victims are mapped to a representational space where dehumanization (absent agency/experience) predicts instrumental violence but not moral violence because moral agents require recognition of human capacities to warrant punishment or harm.

Exposure to objective inequality information shifts two dimensions of causal attribution: increased salience of structural (contextual) factors and decreased salience of individual (effort) factors in explaining economic outcomes. This attribution shift mediates subsequent support for redistributive policies. The mechanism is Bayesian updating of opportunity beliefs in response to environmental signals that contradict American Dream ideology.

Temporary sharing invokes two coupled processes: perceived ephemerality reduces privacy concerns via an appraisal mechanism, which increases disclosure quantity and disinhibition in content. In parallel, observers apply actor-observer attribution bias, attributing visible content to enduring character rather than to situational affordances (platform temporariness). These processes are independent: disclosure happens offline (cognitive appraisal), judgment happens offline (attribution), and they do not share information.

Information search strategy (attribute-based versus alternative-based transitions) predicts and causally influences temporal discounting. Comparative search (attribute-focus) promotes explicit tradeoff representation between amount and delay dimensions, amplifying sensitivity to framing cues. Integrative search (alternative-focus) treats each option holistically and shows reduced framing effects. Manipulating ease of transition types alters both strategy adoption and resulting discount rates.

Cognitive strategy training (negative framing of unhealthy foods or positive framing of healthy foods) alters subjective valuation of food stimuli through craving and willingness-to-pay channels. This revaluation occurs via explicit instruction-guided reflection during stimulus exposure. Training generalizes beyond trained stimuli and persists through subsequent choices without conscious strategy use during choice itself, suggesting the mechanism alters internal value representations rather than requiring online control.

Bayesian social learning in networks: individuals update beliefs about factual trends (Arctic sea ice) by incorporating peer estimates, moving toward higher accuracy when peer estimates carry information orthogonal to baseline bias. Partisan identity priming (party logos or peer political labels) activates identity-protective cognition, reducing the weight given to peer estimates. Salience of identity can suppress or amplify the informativeness of peer communication without changing network topology.
domain: social learning polarization climate science beliefs
